content.description:
The universal data structure in Hobson. Every entity—notes, types, code, views, the viewport itself—is an item with this identical structure.

## Structure

| Field | Type | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| `id` | String | Globally unique identifier (UUID). Seed items use memorable GUIDs like `00000000-...`. User items get random UUIDs. |
| `name` | String? | Optional human-readable identifier. **Required for code items** (used by `api.require()`). Must be unique among code items. |
| `type` | String | UUID of the item's [type](item://11111111-0000-0000-0000-000000000000). Every item must have a type. |
| `extends` | String? | UUID of parent type (for type definitions only). Creates the type hierarchy. |
| `created` | Number | Timestamp (ms since epoch). Set once at creation, never changes. |
| `modified` | Number | Timestamp of last change. Updated automatically on save. |
| `attachments` | Array | Ordered list of attachment references. See Attachments below. |
| `content` | Object | Arbitrary key-value data. The kernel doesn't interpret this—views do. |

## Attachments

The `attachments` array holds references to other items, establishing composition relationships:

```javascript
attachments: [
  { id: "child-uuid", view: { type: "view-uuid", ...viewConfig } }
]
```

Each child object has:
- `id` — The child item's UUID
- `view` — How to display this child (required `type` field plus view-specific config)
- `previousView` — Optional; preserved state for view toggling

For spatial containers, view config includes positioning: `{ x, y, width, height, z, minimized, ... }`

## Content Field

The `content` object is unconstrained—each item type defines its own schema. Common patterns:

- **Notes**: `{ description: "markdown content" }`
- **Code items**: `{ description: "what it does", code: "export function..." }`
- **Types**: `{ description: "what this type represents", required_fields: [...] }`
- **Views**: `{ for_type: "uuid", code: "...", displayName: "..." }`

The `description` field is conventional—every item should have one explaining its purpose.

## Why Uniform Structure?

Radical uniformity enables:
- **One storage system** handles all data
- **One validation system** (type chain checking) applies everywhere
- **One rendering system** displays everything
- **Any item can link to any other** via `item://` URLs
- **Self-documentation**: the system describes itself using itself

## See Also

- [kernel:type-definition](item://11111111-0000-0000-0000-000000000000) — How types categorize items
- [kernel:code](item://22222222-0000-0000-0000-000000000000) — Executable items
- [kernel:view](item://aaaaaaaa-0000-0000-0000-000000000000) — Items that render other items
